    @property
    def max_form_memory_size(self) -> int | None:  # type: ignore[override]
        """The maximum size in bytes that a request form is allowed to
        occupy, before a request stream exception is raised.

        This defaults to 500,000 bytes, or to the value of the
        :data:`MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE` config key if set in the current
        application context. It can be overridden on a per-request basis
        by setting the instance attribute.

        .. versionadded:: 3.1.0
        """
        if hasattr(self, "_max_form_memory_size"):
            return self._max_form_memory_size

        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE"]

        return super().max_form_memory_size  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

    @max_form_memory_size.setter
    def max_form_memory_size(self, value: int | None) -> None:
        self._max_form_memory_size = value

    @property
    def max_form_parts(self) -> int:  # type: ignore[override]
        """The maximum number of multipart parts (fields) to parse from
        a request form. Parsing a request form with more parts will raise
        a request stream exception.

        This defaults to 1,000, or to the value of the
        :data:`MAX_FORM_PARTS` config key if set in the current
        application context. It can be overridden on a per-request basis
        by setting the instance attribute.

        .. versionadded:: 3.1.0
        """
        if hasattr(self, "_max_form_parts"):
            return self._max_form_parts

        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_FORM_PARTS"]

        return super().max_form_parts  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

    @max_form_parts.setter
    def max_form_parts(self, value: int) -> None:
        self._max_form_parts = value

EOF

from flask import Flask, Request

app = Flask(__name__)

# Test 1: Default values from configuration
print("Test 1: Default config values")
print(f"  MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE in config: {app.config['MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE']}")
print(f"  MAX_FORM_PARTS in config: {app.config['MAX_FORM_PARTS']}")

# Test 2: Request class properties outside app context
print("\nTest 2: Request properties outside app context")
req = Request({})
print(f"  Default max_form_memory_size: {req.max_form_memory_size}")
print(f"  Default max_form_parts: {req.max_form_parts}")

# Test 3: Request properties inside app context with default config
print("\nTest 3: Request properties inside app context with default config")
with app.test_request_context():
    req = app.request_class({})
    print(f"  max_form_memory_size: {req.max_form_memory_size}")
    print(f"  max_form_parts: {req.max_form_parts}")

# Test 4: Per-request override
print("\nTest 4: Per-request override")
with app.test_request_context():
    req = app.request_class({})
    req.max_form_memory_size = 1000000
    req.max_form_parts = 500
    print(f"  Overridden max_form_memory_size: {req.max_form_memory_size}")
    print(f"  Overridden max_form_parts: {req.max_form_parts}")
    # Verify we can still access config values if needed
    print(f"  Config MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE still: {app.config['MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE']}")

# Test 5: Config override
print("\nTest 5: Config override")
app.config['MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE'] = 1_000_000
app.config['MAX_FORM_PARTS'] = 500
with app.test_request_context():
    req = app.request_class({})
    print(f"  max_form_memory_size: {req.max_form_memory_size}")
    print(f"  max_form_parts: {req.max_form_parts}")

# Test 6: Per-request override still works after config change
print("\nTest 6: Per-request override still works after config change")
with app.test_request_context():
    req = app.request_class({})
    req.max_form_memory_size = 2_000_000
    req.max_form_parts = 100
    print(f"  Per-request max_form_memory_size: {req.max_form_memory_size}")
    print(f"  Per-request max_form_parts: {req.max_form_parts}")
    print(f"  Config MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE: {app.config['MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE']}")

print("\nAll tests passed!")
EOF

Now let me update the `wrappers.py` file to add property getters and setters for `max_content_length`, `max_form_memory_size`, and `max_form_parts`.

cat > /workspace/repo/src/flask/wrappers.py << 'EOF'
from __future__ import annotations

import typing as t

from werkzeug.exceptions import BadRequest
from werkzeug.exceptions import HTTPException
from werkzeug.wrappers import Request as RequestBase
from werkzeug.wrappers import Response as ResponseBase

from . import json
from .globals import current_app
from .helpers import _split_blueprint_path

if t.TYPE_CHECKING:  # pragma: no cover
    from werkzeug.routing import Rule


class Request(RequestBase):
    """The request object used by default in Flask.  Remembers the
    matched endpoint and view arguments.

    It is what ends up as :class:`~flask.request`.  If you want to replace
    the request object used you can subclass this and set
    :attr:`~flask.Flask.request_class` to your subclass.

    The request object is a :class:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Request` subclass and
    provides all of the attributes Werkzeug defines plus a few Flask
    specific ones.
    """

    json_module: t.Any = json

    #: The internal URL rule that matched the request.  This can be
    #: useful to inspect which methods are allowed for the URL from
    #: a before/after handler (``request.url_rule.methods``) etc.
    #: Though if the request's method was invalid for the URL rule,
    #: the valid list is available in ``routing_exception.valid_methods``
    #: instead (an attribute of the Werkzeug exception
    #: :exc:`~werkzeug.exceptions.MethodNotAllowed`)
    #: because the request was never internally bound.
    #:
    #: .. versionadded:: 0.6
    url_rule: Rule | None = None

    #: A dict of view arguments that matched the request.  If an exception
    #: happened when matching, this will be ``None``.
    view_args: dict[str, t.Any] | None = None

    #: If matching the URL failed, this is the exception that will be
    #: raised / was raised as part of the request handling.  This is
    #: usually a :exc:`~werkzeug.exceptions.NotFound` exception or
    #: something similar.
    routing_exception: HTTPException | None = None

    @property
    def max_content_length(self) -> int | None:  # type: ignore[override]
        """The maximum content length for the request.

        This can be set as a per-request override, which takes precedence
        over the application's configuration. If no per-request value is set
        and the app context is available, it falls back to the
        ``MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH`` configuration. Otherwise, ``None`` is returned
        (no limit) which uses Werkzeug's base behavior.

        See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Request.max_content_length` in
        Werkzeug's docs for more details.

        .. versionadded:: 3.1.0
        """
        if hasattr(self, "_max_content_length"):
            return self._max_content_length  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH"]
        return None

    @max_content_length.setter
    def max_content_length(self, value: int | None) -> None:
        """Set a per-request override for the maximum content length."""
        self._max_content_length = value  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

    @property
    def max_form_memory_size(self) -> int:
        """The maximum form memory size for the request.

        This can be set as a per-request override, which takes precedence
        over the application's configuration. If no per-request value is set
        and the app context is available, it falls back to the
        ``MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE`` configuration. Otherwise, Werkzeug's default
        is used.

        See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Request.max_form_memory_size` in
        Werkzeug's docs for more details.

        .. versionadded:: 3.1.0
        """
        if hasattr(self, "_max_form_memory_size"):
            return self._max_form_memory_size  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_FORM_MEMORY_SIZE"]
        return super().max_form_memory_size  # type: ignore[return-value]

    @max_form_memory_size.setter
    def max_form_memory_size(self, value: int) -> None:
        """Set a per-request override for the maximum form memory size."""
        self._max_form_memory_size = value  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

    @property
    def max_form_parts(self) -> int:
        """The maximum number of form parts for the request.

        This can be set as a per-request override, which takes precedence
        over the application's configuration. If no per-request value is set
        and the app context is available, it falls back to the
        ``MAX_FORM_PARTS`` configuration. Otherwise, Werkzeug's default
        is used.

        See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Request.max_form_parts` in
        Werkzeug's docs for more details.

        .. versionadded:: 3.1.0
        """
        if hasattr(self, "_max_form_parts"):
            return self._max_form_parts  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_FORM_PARTS"]
        return super().max_form_parts  # type: ignore[return-value]

    @max_form_parts.setter
    def max_form_parts(self, value: int) -> None:
        """Set a per-request override for the maximum number of form parts."""
        self._max_form_parts = value  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

    @property
    def endpoint(self) -> str | None:
        """The endpoint that matched the request URL.

        This will be ``None`` if matching failed or has not been
        performed yet.

        This in combination with :attr:`view_args` can be used to
        reconstruct the same URL or a modified URL.
        """
        if self.url_rule is not None:
            return self.url_rule.endpoint  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        return None

    @property
    def blueprint(self) -> str | None:
        """The registered name of the current blueprint.

        This will be ``None`` if the endpoint is not part of a
        blueprint, or if URL matching failed or has not been performed
        yet.

        This does not necessarily match the name the blueprint was
        created with. It may have been nested, or registered with a
        different name.
        """
        endpoint = self.endpoint

        if endpoint is not None and "." in endpoint:
            return endpoint.rpartition(".")[0]

        return None

    @property
    def blueprints(self) -> list[str]:
        """The registered names of the current blueprint upwards through
        parent blueprints.

        This will be an empty list if there is no current blueprint, or
        if URL matching failed.

        .. versionadded:: 2.0.1
        """
        name = self.blueprint

        if name is None:
            return []

        return _split_blueprint_path(name)

    def _load_form_data(self) -> None:
        super()._load_form_data()

        # In debug mode we're replacing the files multidict with an ad-hoc
        # subclass that raises a different error for key errors.
        if (
            current_app
            and current_app.debug
            and self.mimetype != "multipart/form-data"
            and not self.files
        ):

class Response(ResponseBase):
    """The response object that is used by default in Flask.  Works like the
    response object from Werkzeug but is set to have an HTML mimetype by
    default.  Quite often you don't have to create this object yourself because
    :meth:`~flask.Flask.make_response` will take care of that for you.

    If you want to replace the response object used you can subclass this and
    set :attr:`~flask.Flask.response_class` to your subclass.

    .. versionchanged:: 1.0
        JSON support is added to the response, like the request. This is useful
        when testing to get the test client response data as JSON.

    .. versionchanged:: 1.0

        Added :attr:`max_cookie_size`.
    """

    default_mimetype: str | None = "text/html"

    json_module = json

    autocorrect_location_header = False

    @property
    def max_cookie_size(self) -> int:  # type: ignore
        """Read-only view of the :data:`MAX_COOKIE_SIZE` config key.

        See :attr:`~werkzeug.wrappers.Response.max_cookie_size` in
        Werkzeug's docs.
        """
        if current_app:
            return current_app.config["MAX_COOKIE_SIZE"]  # type: ignore[no-any-return]

        # return Werkzeug's default when not in an app context
        return super().max_cookie_size
